name: Foundry Aeterna
classification_notes: The foundry explicitly describes Foundry Aeterna as a neo-grotesque, grounded in modernist and minimalist principles, with closed terminals, a large x-height and rounded letterforms characteristic of the genre.
summary: A neo-grotesque from The Foundry Types spanning nine weights with matching italics, a large x-height and a supplementary set of over 290 icons and symbols.
description: Built on modernist foundations with closed terminals and rounded letterforms, Foundry Aeterna prioritises legibility at smaller sizes through compact spacing and a generous x-height. Distinctive details include a serpentine 'a', a double-storey 'g', a curved 't' crossbar and three distinct ampersand variants, alongside a slashed zero and a figure one with a baseline bar. The accompanying Symbols style provides over 290 icons covering transport, wayfinding and user interface applications, all aligned to the cap height for easy integration.
foundry_blurb: The Foundry Types describe Foundry Aeterna as a decisive, thoughtful interpretation of the neo-grotesque for modern digital typography, conceived in response to the complacency and sameness they see in the genre. Grounded in modernist and minimalist principles, it features harmonious utilitarian letterforms, a large x-height, compact spacing and subtle contemporary details such as slightly extended terminals and horizontal shaping on selected characters. A Symbols set of over 290 icons accompanies the typeface, and a Variable Font is included with complete Roman, Italic or Family purchases.

name: Architype Motherwell
classification_notes: A geometric stencil display typeface derived from Wim Crouwel's lettering for a 1966 exhibition poster, characterised by a strict grid system, gothic-influenced construction and stencil gaps throughout all weights.
summary: A geometric stencil display family drawn from Wim Crouwel's 1966 exhibition poster lettering for Robert Motherwell at the Stedelijk Museum, Amsterdam.
description: Based on Crouwel's narrow grid system from his Motherwell poster, the family runs across five weights from Light to Heavy, with stencil gaps maintained at a consistent thickness throughout to keep transitions smooth. Alternate lowercase glyphs, extended ligatures, positive and negative circled figures and a Bitcoin symbol extend the character set beyond the original lettering. The gothic-influenced construction and tight geometric discipline make it best suited to editorial display, cultural institutions and poster work.
foundry_blurb: The Foundry Types describe Architype Motherwell as a powerful display stencil design inspired by Wim Crouwel's iconic 1966 poster for Robert Motherwell's exhibition at the Stedelijk Museum. They note the challenges of adapting Crouwel's narrow grid to a full weight range, including raising the ascender height for diacritics and balancing stencil gap thickness across five weights. The family includes alternative lowercase glyphs, positive and negative circled figures, arrows, a Bitcoin symbol and extended ligatures.

name: Architype Ingenieur
classification_notes: A strictly grid-based geometric construction with 45-degree angles replacing curves, rooted in Crouwel's engineered letterform system from the 1960s. Its dot matrix variant and angular geometry place it firmly in display territory rather than conventional geometric sans.
summary: A grid-based geometric family by Wim Crouwel, formalised from his 1960s exhibition lettering for the Stedelijk Museum and Venice Biennale catalogues.
description: Developed in collaboration with Wim Crouwel and released in 2012, the design codifies his system of 45-degree angled strokes replacing conventional curves, first seen in exhibition catalogues and posters from 1960 onwards. The four-style family runs Light, Regular, Bold and a Dot variant that references the dot matrix adaptations Crouwel made for posters such as the 1964 Job Hansen Fodor Museum piece. The name derives from Crouwel's own words: 'I am an engineer, troubled by aesthetics.'
foundry_blurb: The Foundry Types describe Architype Ingenieur as one of the last hands-on typeface collaborations with Wim Crouwel, created in 2011 and released in 2012. They trace its origins to Crouwel's 'Olanda' letterforms of the 1960s, shaped by his boyhood fascination with naval warship lettering and developed across Stedelijk Museum catalogues for exhibitions including Brusselmans in 1960 and Mataré in 1964. The clean, geometrically constructed grid system transmits what they describe as a sharp but subtle engineered aesthetic.

name: Architype Renner
classification_notes: A geometric sans rooted in Paul Renner's experimental drawings for Futura, featuring pure circular forms, minimal stroke contrast and classical Roman proportions, placing it squarely in the geometric sans tradition.
summary: A 1997 revival of Paul Renner's experimental Futura drawings, including letterforms never released by the Bauer Foundry in the late 1920s.
description: Part of The Foundry's Architype One series, this family reconstructs the geometric glyphs Renner drew during Futura's development between 1924 and 1930, alongside alternate letterforms that Bauer suppressed before publication. Four weights from Regular to Bold carry old-style figures and the sharp, pure geometric details of Renner's original vision. It is one of the few typefaces that lets you work directly with the pre-commercial Futura, before optical compromises softened its most radical characters.
foundry_blurb: The Foundry describe Architype Renner as one of the first typefaces in their Architype One series, released in 1997 to recreate the experimental geometric glyphs Paul Renner drew for Futura, including letterforms never originally issued by the Bauer Foundry between 1927 and 1930. They note Renner's starting point was classical Roman inscriptional capitals, and that the family features old-style figures and experimental alternatives alongside the pure geometric forms and minimal stroke contrast that define Futura's enduring quality.

name: Fernhout
classification_notes: Constructed from a strict rectangular grid of two-wide by three-high units, with circular segments and angled indentations forming each glyph. The system is entirely modular and geometric, placing it firmly in the display category as a constructed alphabet rather than a text typeface.
summary: A digital revival of Wim Crouwel's 1963 modular lettering for the Van Abbemuseum's Edgar Fernhout exhibition, expanded into a complete typeface by The Foundry Types.
description: Originally a single-use poster title designed by Crouwel for a 1963 Van Abbemuseum exhibition, the letterforms were built on a rectangular grid system with circular segments and angled indentations. The Foundry Types completed the alphabet, solving the harder diagonal glyphs without departing from Crouwel's original logic. It won a Type Directors Club Award for Typographic Excellence when used for Dutch Design Week 2021 in collaboration with Thonik.
foundry_blurb: Commissioned for an Edgar Fernhout exhibition poster in 1963, Wim Crouwel devised a rectangular block grid two units wide by three units tall, with circled segments and angled indentations forming each character. Crouwel acknowledged inspiration from abstract landscapes and Dutch painters, and never intended the lettering to become a full typeface. The Foundry Types extended the system into a complete alphabet and released it as a single-weight digital font.

name: Foundry Arkias
classification_notes: Inspired by the simplified letterforms found in mid-century Swiss pharmaceutical packaging, Foundry Arkias uses a vertical axis, modular dome-like arched forms and narrow proportions characteristic of geometric grotesque typography rooted in the International Typographic Style.
summary: A 39-style geometric sans drawn from mid-century Swiss pharmaceutical packaging, with modular arched forms and pinched connecting strokes across thirteen weights.
description: Taking its visual cues from the simplified letterforms Andreas His designed for J. R. Geigy A.G. packaging in the 1950s, Foundry Arkias is not a revival but a modern interpretation built around a vertical axis, narrow proportions and distinctive dome-like arched terminals. Each of the thirteen weights ships with a standard italic and a Recline variant, and a variable font is included with complete family purchases. Alternative glyphs for key characters, circled numbers and an extended ligature set including a 'www' ligature add flexibility for branding and display work.
foundry_blurb: The Foundry Types describe Foundry Arkias as a modern interpretation inspired by the simplified letterforms of mid-century Geigy packaging design, not a copy but something new. The family spans 39 styles across thirteen weights from Hairline to Heavy, each with Italic and Recline variants, and is available as a variable font on complete family purchases. They emphasise its modular arched forms, large x-height and alternative glyphs as tools for typographic creativity across branding, print, screen and mobile applications.

name: Foundry Context
classification_notes: The foundry explicitly describes it as neo-grotesque in style, with minimal stroke contrast, very round pure letterforms, and no humanistic script influence, placing it firmly in the neo-grotesque tradition.
summary: A neo-grotesque sans with very round, minimal-contrast letterforms designed to sit comfortably across branding, print and screen applications.
description: Drawing on 19th-century sans serif precedents, Foundry Context keeps stroke contrast low and letterforms deliberately unrefined, avoiding both humanistic script influence and over-conscious detail. Its large x-height, compact spacing and even terminal distribution across the weight range make it a practical choice for legibility-critical work. The six-style family runs from Regular to Extra Bold with a single italic, and includes small caps, ligatures, tabular and proportional figures.
foundry_blurb: The Foundry describes Context as a 'no-nonsense' sans designed to be universal across many contexts, reminiscent of 19th-century grotesques with round, pure letterforms and minimal stroke contrast in the neo-grotesque style. Created after Foundry Monoline as a deliberate counterpoint, it was conceived to be extremely neutral and effortless, with a low-contrast appearance and sympathetic shapes that offer an understated tone of voice suited to a variety of branding sectors and print or screen applications.

name: Foundry Dat
classification_notes: Foundry Dat is built on a 5x9 dot matrix grid using oblong lozenge shapes, with an integrated background grid that aligns when set solid. Its primary function is graphic and compositional rather than text-setting, placing it firmly in the display category.
summary: A dot matrix display face built on a 5x9 lozenge grid, designed to layer and tile into continuous patterns and compositional shapes.
description: Constructed on a strict 5x9 grid of oblong lozenge shapes, Foundry Dat carries an integrated background grid that aligns seamlessly when text is set solid, allowing characters to form repeating patterns when stacked or offset. It pairs with the companion family Foundry Dit, which shares the same letterforms but without the background grid, functioning instead as a typewriter-style correspondence face. Four weights run from Light to Bold, with extended dash characters to continue the grid horizontally and vertically.
foundry_blurb: The Foundry describe Foundry Dat as part of a dot matrix grid system concept shared with Foundry Plek and Flek. Its integrated background grid aligns vertically and horizontally when set solid, and characters can be offset to build compositional shapes, turning text into imagery. Named in Dutch, Dit and Dat means This and That, coined in response to naming the Plek and Flek families.

name: Foundry Dit
classification_notes: Built on a 5x9 dot matrix grid using oblong lozenge shapes, Foundry Dit is a display typeface with a typewriter and dot-matrix character that makes it unsuitable for running text but distinctive as a graphic system.
summary: A dot-matrix display face built on a 5x9 lozenge grid, designed to layer with its companion Foundry Dat for typographic pattern-making.
description: Each letterform sits on a strict oblong dot-matrix grid of soft, padded lozenge shapes, giving the face a typewriter quality when set as running text. The family pairs with Foundry Dat, which adds a matching grid background so the two can be superimposed precisely to build colour schemes and compositional patterns. Four weights run from Light to Bold, each including extension dashes to continue the grid horizontally and vertically.
foundry_blurb: The Foundry describe Dit as sharing the same integral dot matrix grid concept as Foundry Plek and Flek, using oblong lozenge shapes on a 5x9 grid that allows precise layering with companion family Foundry Dat. Dit functions as a legible correspondence font with a typewriter feel, while Dat adds a grid background that forms continuous patterns when set solid. The name Dit and Dat — Dutch for This and That — came from an off-the-cuff remark during design. The family was featured on the cover of Graphics International 103 in February 2003.

name: Foundry Fabriek
classification_notes: A stencil typeface derived from Foundry Gridnik, built on a systematic grid with deliberate stencil breaks and vertical stress. Its industrial fabrication origins and architectural applications place it firmly in the display/stencil category.
summary: A stencil sans designed by David Quay, originating as a commission to laser-cut poems into steel panels at the Interpolis building in Tilburg, with Wim Crouwel as consultant.
description: David Quay developed Foundry Fabriek from Foundry Gridnik after being commissioned to create letterforms for steel panels in an Interpolis office interior, working under the guidance of Wim Crouwel. The design resolves the tension between stencil geometry and diagonal strokes by adding a straight on the inside of terminals, giving it a considered finish that lifts it beyond purely mechanical construction. Five weights from Light to Extra Bold make it flexible for architectural signage, environmental graphics and display work where letterforms are cast, cut or embossed at scale.
foundry_blurb: The Foundry describe Foundry Fabriek as a stencil typeface rooted in industrial fabrication, with its name and systematic grid reflecting the assembly of components into a unified structure. Originally designed by David Quay at the recommendation of Wim Crouwel for an Interpolis office interior, the brief called for stencil letterforms laser-cut into large steel panels to display a series of poems. The design balances machine-made precision with a human touch through thoughtful stencil breaks and a five-weight progression suited to architectural and signage applications.

name: Foundry Flek
classification_notes: Foundry Flek is built entirely on a 5x9 dot matrix grid system, with the background grid visible behind the letterforms, placing it firmly in the display category as a decorative, system-grid typeface.
summary: A dot-matrix display typeface built on a strict 5x9 grid, with a visible background dot pattern that aligns perfectly when set solid to create continuous graphic fields.
description: Each letterform in Foundry Flek is constructed from circular dots on a 5x9 grid, with the background matrix retained behind the letters. When set solid, the dots align both vertically and horizontally to form a seamless repeating pattern across a text block. As weights increase from Light to Bold, the dot centres remain fixed, allowing weights to be mixed and layered with precision for multi-colour or dimensional effects.
foundry_blurb: The Foundry describe Flek as a companion to Foundry Plek, sharing the same 5x9 dot matrix grid letterforms but distinguished by its visible background grid. Both families include an auxiliary font containing only the dot matrix, and the dots are designed to vary in size while remaining strictly grid-aligned, enabling designers to overlay text boxes and build up colour schemes. The families were featured in Design Week, Grafik and Graphics International, and are licensed exclusively through Monotype.

name: Foundry Form Sans
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a modern humanistic sans, with open terminals, shallow connecting junctions and a strong horizontal axis that lend it a distinctly humanist character despite its clean angularity.
summary: A slightly condensed humanist sans with a pronounced horizontal axis, designed to pair with Foundry Form Serif across shared capital and x-height proportions.
description: Drawn concurrently with Foundry Form Serif, this family shares matching capital and x-height proportions with its serif counterpart so the two can be used together without friction. The defining characteristic is a maximum horizontal axis achieved through shallow connecting junctions and open terminals that finish at near-ninety-degree angles to the vertical, giving the alphabet a low wave-like rhythm. Seven styles run from Book to Extra Bold with matching italics, and the set includes small caps, ligatures, lining and old-style figures in both tabular and proportional spacing.
foundry_blurb: The Foundry describe Foundry Form Sans as a modern humanistic sans and the counterpoint to Foundry Form Serif, both drawn concurrently with matching capital and x-height proportions. The central concept was to achieve the maximum possible horizontal axis, evident in the shallow connecting junctions of letters such as a, b, d and n, and in open terminals that cut at a sharp near-ninety-degree angle to the vertical. The result is described as economical with space, highly legible at small sizes and suited to both headline and text settings.

name: Foundry Form Serif
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a modern Egyptian style typeface with wedge-shaped serifs and strong horizontal axis, placing it firmly in the slab/Egyptian subcategory with notable stroke contrast.
summary: A modern Egyptian slab with wedge serifs and a pronounced horizontal axis, drawn to work alongside Foundry Form Sans at matching cap and x-height proportions.
description: The typeface's defining characteristic is a deliberately shallow connecting junction across letters such as 'a', 'h', 'm' and 'n', creating a strong lateral rhythm across the line. Slightly condensed proportions keep it economical, and the open counters with near ninety-degree terminal angles aid legibility at small sizes and on screen. Seven styles run from Book to Extra Bold with cursive italics through the lighter weights, making it suitable for both editorial text and headline work.
foundry_blurb: The Foundry describe Foundry Form Serif as a modern Egyptian style typeface conceived concurrently with Foundry Form Sans, both drawn with matching capital and x-height proportions so the two families work harmoniously together. The central design ambition was to maximise the horizontal axis, producing shallow junctions and open terminals that finish at sharp angles to the vertical, lending the whole alphabet a flowing, directional energy suited to screen and print text setting.

name: Foundry Gridnik
classification_notes: A strictly grid-based geometric sans with angular, modular letterforms derived from Crouwel's systematic unit-based construction, placing it firmly in the geometric sans category with a distinctly rationalist character.
summary: A digitisation of Wim Crouwel's unpublished Olivetti typewriter face from the late 1960s, built on a strict modular unit system and expanded into a 10-style family by The Foundry in 1996.
description: Derived from Crouwel's Olivetti commission and his pen and ink drawings for 1976 Dutch PPT postage stamps, the design applies a rigorous grid logic in which every weight is a calculated multiple of a base unit of seven. The result is an angular geometric sans with an unusual warmth that separates it from colder modular faces. Four weights from Light to Extra Bold each carry an italic, all developed with Crouwel's direct approval.
foundry_blurb: The Foundry describe Foundry Gridnik as a remarkable, distinctive and angular geometric design by Dutch visionary Wim Crouwel, once called 'the thinking man's Courier'. Created in close collaboration with Crouwel following a 1996 agreement, the family takes its name from his nickname 'Mr. Gridnik', a tribute to his devotion to grids and systems, and is based on his original Olivetti typewriter concept alongside drawings for the Dutch PPT postage stamps.

name: Foundry Journal
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a humanistic sans serif; derived from ITC Quay Sans with flares removed, leaving clean skeletal forms with sharp cut-in junctions that place it firmly in the humanist sans tradition.
summary: A humanist sans drawn by David Quay in 1995 as a stripped-back reworking of his earlier ITC Quay Sans, optimised for narrow column measures in magazines and journals.
description: Designed by David Quay and released in 1995, it began as a wholesale redrawing of ITC Quay Sans, removing the original's flared terminals to expose a leaner skeletal structure with sharp cut-in junctions at connecting strokes. The result is a near-monoweight face with enough internal rhythm to sustain long runs of body text without monotony. Five styles run from Book to Bold with a single Book Italic, making it a compact but functional choice for print editorial work.
foundry_blurb: The Foundry describe Foundry Journal as a humanistic sans serif reworking of ITC Quay Sans, originally designed by David Quay before The Foundry was established. On revisiting the design, Quay removed the fine flared terminals, judging them inconsequential to the core structure, and completely redrew the entire typeface. The result is a legible, economical face suited to the narrow column measures typical of magazines, journals and publications, with sharp cut-in junctions that aid readability at smaller sizes.

name: Foundry Monoline
classification_notes: A structured, grid-based geometric sans with a single-thickness stroke appearance, placing it firmly in the geometric tradition. Its squarish letterforms and clean linear aesthetic distinguish it from humanist or grotesque approaches.
summary: A grid-constructed geometric sans with a consistent single-thickness stroke, designed for branding and editorial use across six weights with matching italics.
description: Conceived in 2000 after the designer observed shop signage in Wiesbaden, Germany, Foundry Monoline uses a structured grid system to produce letterforms that appear to maintain a single uniform stroke weight throughout. The family runs from Ultra Light to Extra Bold with corresponding italics, giving twelve styles in total. The outer weights are particularly effective at large display sizes, where the optical refinements that maintain the illusion of monolinear consistency become most visible.
foundry_blurb: The Foundry Types describe Foundry Monoline as an elegant, modern and well-crafted typeface suited to branding, promotional and advertising work. The clean, linear appearance was achieved through a structured grid system with careful optical adjustments to simulate a single-thickness stroke. The concept originated in 2000 when designer David Quay, living in Wiesbaden, Germany, began sketching a squarish typeface on a paper napkin, responding to the unsympathetic signage he encountered daily.

name: Foundry Old Style
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a transitional roman in the classical tradition, drawing on incunabula typefaces and the pen stroke influence of Nicolas Jenson, placing it firmly in the humanist old style serif lineage.
summary: A humanist old style serif by David Quay, drawn initially with a broken clarinet reed and ink, tracing its lineage to the incunabula types of Nicolas Jenson.
description: Released in 1990 as The Foundry's debut typeface, it began as freehand calligraphy before being redrawn repeatedly onto detail and tracing paper, then digitised using Ikarus software. The pen stroke structure is present but more restrained than its early printing forebears, sitting closer to the Garamonds in refinement. A small family of four styles covering Normal, Normal Italic, Medium and Bold, intended for classical book work and positioned as a text companion to Foundry Sans.
foundry_blurb: The Foundry describe Foundry Old Style as a transitional roman in the classical tradition, showing the influence of the pen stroke as seen in incunabula printed types. Inspired by Nicolas Jenson, David Quay began the letterforms as calligraphy using a broken clarinet reed and fountain pen ink, before he and Freda Sack redrew them many times and digitised the result using Ikarus software. Originally conceived as a text face with a small weight range for classical book work, it is positioned as a pairing companion to Foundry Sans.

name: Foundry Origin
classification_notes: Described explicitly as an Egyptian slab serif, with thick, carefully graded serifs across all weights and a strong horizontal dynamic typical of the Egyptian genre, combined with modern proportions and a generous x-height.
summary: A slab serif by Stuart de Rozario designed from the outset for legibility on screen and in print, with deliberately thickened serifs graded smoothly across seven weights.
description: Designed in 2006-07 when screen rendering of thin serif strokes was still unreliable, Foundry Origin takes an Egyptian approach with serifs substantial enough to hold their shape at small sizes on low-resolution displays. The family runs from Light to Extra Bold with a single italic at Book weight, and the character set includes small capitals, old-style and tabular figures, and ligatures. Its generous x-height and considered proportions make it well suited to editorial, signage and information design.
foundry_blurb: The Foundry Types describe Foundry Origin as a robust yet elegant, classical but contemporary slab serif with an elemental quality that hints at its Egyptian roots. With a generous x-height and modern proportions, they say it is essentially legible at small point sizes and versatile at larger sizes, making it ideal for editorial, signage and information design.

name: Foundry Plek
classification_notes: Foundry Plek is built entirely on a 5x9 dot matrix grid system where circular dot letterforms scale in weight while keeping dot centres fixed. This places it firmly in the display/dot matrix category, designed for layering, pattern-making and graphic experimentation rather than body text.
summary: A dot matrix display typeface built on a strict 5x9 grid, designed for layering weights and colours to create dimensional, pattern-based typographic effects.
description: Each letterform is constructed from circular dots constrained to a 5x9 grid, with dot centres held in a fixed position across all four weights so that Light, Regular, Medium and Bold can be overlaid precisely. This fixed registration allows different colours to be applied within the same text block, producing a layered, dimensional result. An auxiliary grid font ships with the family, letting designers extend the dot matrix pattern beyond the letters themselves.
foundry_blurb: The Foundry describe Plek as a dot matrix typeface built on a 5x9 grid system where dots vary in size across weights but remain anchored to their grid positions. This allows different weights to be set in exact correspondence, enabling layered colour effects and dimensional typographic compositions. It functions as a companion to Foundry Flek, which adds the same background grid matrix to the letterforms, and both families include an auxiliary font for building custom grid patterns.

name: Foundry Sans
classification_notes: Explicitly described as a humanist sans serif, its proportions drawn from Stempel Garamond and influenced by calligraphic letterforms with double-storied 'a' and 'g', following old-style text typeface principles.
summary: A humanist sans serif by David Quay and Freda Sack, drawn from the proportions of Stempel Garamond and shaped by a conversation with Hans Eduard Meyer at ATypI Paris in 1989.
description: Originally released in 1990 as The Foundry's first sans serif, Foundry Sans takes its proportions from Stempel Garamond in the same way Meyer's Syntax drew on Sabon. The double-storied lowercase 'a' and 'g', low-contrast thick-and-thin strokes, and calligraphic underpinning give it a warmth uncommon in workhorse humanist sans families. Nine styles now span Light through Extra Bold with italic cuts at Normal, Medium and Demi.
foundry_blurb: The Foundry describe Foundry Sans as a humanist sans serif designed by David Quay and developed with Freda Sack, inspired by a 1989 conversation with Hans Eduard Meyer at ATypI in Paris. Meyer explained that Sabon was the inspiration behind his Syntax, which prompted The Foundry to base Foundry Sans on the proportions of Stempel Garamond. Originally four weights with a single italic, the family has since grown to nine styles across Light to Extra Bold.

name: Foundry Sterling
classification_notes: Foundry Sterling draws on the humanist sans tradition of Gill, Granby and Johnston, with open letterforms and classical proportions that place it firmly in the early 20th-century British humanist lineage.
summary: A British humanist sans in seven weights, rooted in the Gill and Johnston tradition and developed from corporate typeface commissions for Railtrack and British Gas around 1999.
description: Developed by Freda Sack and Stuart de Rozario from earlier corporate work, Foundry Sterling draws on the open, classical proportions of early 20th-century British humanist sans designs such as Gill, Granby and Johnston. The seven-weight range runs from Light to Extra Bold, with a single italic at the Book weight, and the family is designed to hold up equally in text, identity and signage contexts. Its clean colour in text setting and unfussy stroke quality make it a steady choice for editorial and wayfinding work where restraint is the priority.
foundry_blurb: The Foundry Types describe Foundry Sterling as a functional and eloquent typeface family created to bring a quintessentially English flavour to modern sans design. The letterforms were drawn with attention to classical proportion and purity of form, referencing humanist types of the early 20th century including Gill, Granby and Johnston. The family grew out of corporate typeface projects for Railtrack and British Gas in 1999, with Stuart de Rozario joining Freda Sack to develop the design into a seven-weight release suited to identity, editorial and signage use.

name: Foundry Tiento
classification_notes: Foundry Tiento is a Neo-classical Didone with strong thick-thin contrast, unbracketed hairline serifs and a vertical stress axis, placing it firmly in the Bodoni and Didot tradition of the late 18th and early 19th century.
summary: A Didone with extreme thick-thin contrast and ball terminals by David Quay, developed over more than a decade and named after classical Spanish tiento music.
description: Rooted in a condensed Didone Quay originally drew for Letraset in 1987, Foundry Tiento takes the same dramatic stroke contrast into more classical proportions, with unbracketed hairline serifs and a vertical axis drawn in the manner of Bodoni and Didot. Four weights from Regular to Heavy each carry a matching italic, and the extreme contrast of the letterforms is preserved across the range whilst the hairline stroke remains constant. The character set covers Central and Eastern European languages with small caps, extended ligatures and four figure sets.
foundry_blurb: The Foundry Types describe Foundry Tiento as a graceful, curvaceous Neo-classical typeface with strong contrasting thick and thin strokes, unbracketed hairline serifs and a vertical axis. Conceived during David Quay's time teaching in Barcelona in 2008, and drawing on the dramatic Didone characteristics of Bodoni and Didot, the family takes its name from classical tiento music Quay encountered during his time in Spain.

name: Foundry Unie
classification_notes: Explicitly geometric in construction, drawing from Futura, Avenir and the Bauhaus/De Stijl tradition, with a large x-height and open terminals. The foundry acknowledges humanist underpinnings but the primary classification is geometric sans.
summary: A geometric sans rooted in the Bauhaus and De Stijl tradition, designed primarily for screen use across ten weights with matching italics.
description: Drawn in reference to Futura, Avenir and Tschichold's Universal alphabet, the typeface was created to serve as The Foundry Types' own website font when the studio relaunched in 2020. Its large x-height, low contrast and open terminals give it an even colour across the weight range, from Thin through to Heavy. The result is a clean, unobtrusive typeface suited to branding, screen interfaces and small-size setting.
foundry_blurb: The Foundry Types describe Foundry Unie as born from pan-European ideals of fraternity and the modernist 'interbellen' period, citing Jan Tschichold's Universal alphabet, Paul Renner's Futura and Adrian Frutiger's Avenir as key references. Named after the De Stijl-era Unie café in Rotterdam, it was designed for screen use with a large x-height, open terminals and a neutral, calming character suited to branding across print, screen and mobile.

name: Foundry Wilson
classification_notes: A revival of Alexander Wilson's 1760 types, placing it firmly in the transitional period between old-style and modern serifs; the foundry explicitly describes it as a transitional serif design with incised letterform characteristics of its era.
summary: A careful revival of Alexander Wilson's 1760 Scottish types, drawn for book work with small capitals, ligatures and old-style figures included.
description: Based on types cast by Alexander Wilson of Glasgow in 1760, this revival redraws the original roman and italic faithfully before extending the family with a Medium and Bold. Wilson's types were known for their even colour and vigorous fit at smaller sizes, qualities that carry through here and make the face well-suited to long-form book typography. The character set adds small capitals, ligatures, old-style figures and a set of printer's flowers drawn from the same historical source.
foundry_blurb: The Foundry describes Foundry Wilson as an elegant but lively serif with beautiful colour and texture on the printed page, citing its origins in Alexander Wilson's 1760 types produced for the Foulis brothers at Glasgow University Press. They note that Wilson's vigorousness was unprecedented in mid-18th-century type design, and that the first bold-face types can be traced back to the Wilson foundry. The revival was lovingly redrawn by The Foundry's designers, with the original roman and italic faithfully reproduced and weights added to complete the family.

name: New Alphabet
classification_notes: Conceived as a radical theoretical exercise constrained entirely to horizontals, verticals and 45-degree angles derived from cathode ray tube dot-matrix technology, placing it firmly in the experimental display category with no conventional typographic antecedent.
summary: Wim Crouwel's 1967 theoretical typeface, built entirely from horizontals, verticals and 45-degree angles in response to early cathode ray tube phototypesetting technology.
description: Designed within the strict constraints of a 5-by-9 pixel grid, the letterforms abandon conventional distinction between upper and lowercase, using a bar above a letter to signal a capital instead. Crouwel never intended the design for practical use; it was a provocation and a point of departure, intended to stimulate debate about how letterforms might adapt to electronic reproduction rather than fight against it. The family has been realised as four styles and is licensed exclusively through Monotype.
foundry_blurb: The Foundry Types describe New Alphabet as Wim Crouwel's most radical experiment, created in close collaboration with him as a four-style family. Conceived in the late 1960s in response to early electronic phototypesetting, it was built around the constraints of cathode ray tube technology, using only horizontals, verticals and 45-degree angles on a 5-by-9 grid. Crouwel intended it as a theoretical exercise to stimulate discussion rather than practical use, though it generated considerable controversy and public debate among his peers.

name: Architype Catalogue Soft Solid
classification_notes: Grid-based letterforms with deliberately rounded, 'padded' outlines derived from Crouwel's experimental Stedelijk museum catalogue work; firmly display in intent with no text-weight application.
summary: A single-weight display typeface drawn from Wim Crouwel's 1970 Stedelijk museum catalogue for a Claes Oldenburg exhibition, with soft, padded letterforms that mimic the sculptor's own aesthetic.
description: Originally cut by hand from pink paper by Crouwel to reflect Oldenburg's soft sculpture work, the typeface was digitised by The Foundry in collaboration with Crouwel following a 1996 agreement. The letterforms are built on Crouwel's characteristic strict grid but given rounded, inflated counters and edges that distinguish it sharply from his harder geometric work. A companion outline variant, Architype Catalogue Soft Outline, is available separately.
foundry_blurb: Part of The Foundry's Crouwel Collection, a series of digital fonts created in close collaboration with Wim Crouwel from 1996 onwards. The soft padded letterforms were originally designed for the Stedelijk museum's 1970 Claes Oldenburg exhibition catalogue, with Crouwel describing the design as a direct response to Oldenburg's soft objects. Oldenburg himself was so taken with the typeface that he asked Crouwel to complete the full alphabet.

name: Architype Catalogue Soft Outline
classification_notes: A digitised revival of Crouwel's grid-based, softly padded letterforms originally cut by hand for a 1970 Stedelijk exhibition catalogue; its rounded, inflated forms place it firmly in the display/experimental category.
summary: A digitised outline version of the softly inflated letterforms Wim Crouwel hand-cut in pink paper for the Stedelijk Museum's 1970 Claes Oldenburg exhibition catalogue.
description: Originally conceived to evoke the tactile quality of Oldenburg's soft sculptures, the alphabet was cut by Crouwel from pink paper and later digitised by The Foundry following a 1996 collaboration agreement with the designer. The letterforms sit on a strict grid yet carry a rounded, padded character entirely at odds with Crouwel's usual geometric precision, making this one of the more unexpected pieces in the Architype collection. Licenced exclusively through Monotype and available via Fonts.com, FontShop, Linotype and MyFonts.
foundry_blurb: Part of The Foundry's Crouwel collection, developed in close collaboration with Wim Crouwel from 1996, digitising his experimental alphabets. Architype Catalogue Soft Outline derives from letterforms Crouwel originally designed for the Stedelijk Museum's 1970 Claes Oldenburg exhibition, inspired directly by the sculptor's soft objects, which Crouwel said 'get into your system'. Oldenburg was so taken with the catalogue typeface that he asked Crouwel to complete the full alphabet, which Crouwel cut from pink paper and assembled by hand.

name: Architype Fodor
classification_notes: Derived from Crouwel's radical grid-based alphabet designed for the Museum Fodor magazine, constructed from a dot matrix title system to produce digital-effect letterforms. The monospaced, constructivist geometry places it firmly in the display category.
summary: A digital revival of Wim Crouwel's 1972 dot-matrix alphabet for the Museum Fodor magazine, built on strict grid logic and a monospaced framework.
description: Drawn from Crouwel's original letterforms for the Museum Fodor, Amsterdam, where cost constraints led him to design type for an electric typewriter, producing an inherently monospaced rhythm. The letterforms were constructed from a dot matrix title system overlaid on a pink-and-orange page grid, giving each character its mechanical, digital-effect character. Part of The Foundry's Crouwel Collection, developed in direct collaboration with Crouwel from 1996.
foundry_blurb: Architype Fodor is part of The Foundry's Crouwel Collection, a set of typefaces developed in close collaboration with Wim Crouwel following his 1996 agreement to create digital fonts from his experimental alphabets. The letterforms originated in work Crouwel designed for the Museum Fodor magazine in Amsterdam in 1972–73, conceived for reproduction on an electric typewriter and structured around a dot matrix title system and a printed dot grid.

name: Architype Stedelijk
classification_notes: Derived from Crouwel's 1968 hand-drawn letterforms built on a strict 57×41 grid, resulting in a machine-like geometric construction with no conventional calligraphic influence; classified as display due to its highly experimental, grid-constrained structure unsuited to continuous text.
summary: A digital revival of Wim Crouwel's 1968 Vormgevers poster alphabet, built on a rigid 57×41-line grid that gives every letterform a deliberately mechanical appearance.
description: Crouwel originally drew the typeface by hand for the Stedelijk Museum's Vormgevers poster in 1968, using a strict grid of 57 vertical and 41 horizontal lines as the sole constructive logic. The result is a letterform system that looks machine-made despite being entirely hand-rendered, and the iconic black-and-white poster with its visible grid became a landmark of Dutch modernism. The Foundry digitised it in collaboration with Crouwel from 1996 as part of a broader effort to preserve his experimental alphabets.
foundry_blurb: The Foundry developed Architype Stedelijk as part of the Crouwel Collection, a series of digital typefaces created in close collaboration with Wim Crouwel following an agreement in 1996 to translate his experimental alphabets into digital fonts. Stedelijk originated in the 1968 Vormgevers poster for the Stedelijk Museum Amsterdam, where Crouwel used a rigid grid of 57 vertical by 41 horizontal lines to construct letterforms that, though hand-drawn, appear machine-made. The poster is considered one of Crouwel's most iconic designs.

name: Architype Vierkant
classification_notes: Derived from Crouwel's radical grid-based letterforms originally created for a 1972 Drupa catalogue, the face sits firmly in experimental/display territory with its rigid modular construction rooted in his 'new alphabet' philosophy.
summary: A digital revival of Wim Crouwel's grid-based letterforms first drawn for a 1972 Drupa catalogue, developed by The Foundry in collaboration with Crouwel from 1996.
description: Constructed from the few letterforms Crouwel sketched for a single spread in the 1972 Drupa catalogue under the theme 'typo vision international', the face reflects the same strict modular logic underpinning his controversial New Alphabet and Fodor typeface. The Foundry developed it digitally as part of a broader Crouwel collection agreed in 1996, grounding each design in his reputation for radical, grid-based work produced for the Van Abbe and Stedelijk museums between 1954 and 1972. Available in a single style, it is primarily a document of experimental typography rather than a workhorse text face.
foundry_blurb: Architype Vierkant is part of The Foundry's Crouwel collection, a series of digital typefaces created in close collaboration with Wim Crouwel following an agreement in 1996 to digitise his experimental alphabets. It was developed from letterforms Crouwel drew for the opening spread of a 1972 Drupa catalogue, on the theme 'typo vision international', revealing the interplay between ideas behind his New Alphabet and Fodor designs.

name: Architype Albers
classification_notes: A revival of Josef Albers's 1926 grid-based alphabet, constructed entirely from geometric primitives: the square, triangle and circle arcs. The stencil-like construction and avant-garde origins place it firmly in display and experimental categories.
summary: A revival of Josef Albers's 1926 grid-based alphabet, reducing letterforms to squares, triangles and circular arcs in a stencil-like construction drawn from Bauhaus ideals.
description: Part of The Foundry Types' Architype Konstrukt collection, this face reconstructs Albers's 1926 experimental alphabet published in Offset, Buch und Werbekunst. Letters are built strictly from geometric primitives, producing an open stencil quality that reflects the inter-war European modernist drive to reduce form to its barest elements. The character set is limited by design, as the experimental source material intended geometry over comprehensive coverage.
foundry_blurb: The Foundry Types describe Architype Albers as part of their Architype Konstrukt collection, a set of avant-garde typefaces derived from the work of artists and designers of the inter-war years whose ideals shaped European modernist design philosophy. This face specifically draws on Josef Albers's 1926 attempts to build an alphabet from purely geometric elements: the square, triangle and parts of a circle, resulting in an unusual stencil-effect typeface. Character sets may be limited due to the experimental nature of the source material.

name: Architype Aubette
classification_notes: Based on Theo van Doesburg's 1928 De Stijl signage lettering, the typeface is a geometric, avant-garde display face rooted in constructivist principles with strictly rectilinear forms.
summary: A digital revival of Theo van Doesburg's 1928 signage lettering for the Café Aubette in Strasbourg, one of the landmark projects of Dutch De Stijl.
description: Part of The Foundry Types' Architype Konstrukt collection, this face is drawn directly from Van Doesburg's hand-lettered signage for the Café Aubette, a collaborative interior project with Jean and Sophie Arp that stood as a high-water mark of 1920s avant-garde theory. The strictly geometric, rectilinear character set reflects De Stijl's ideological constraints, and as with most typefaces in the Konstrukt series, the character set is limited by the experimental nature of its source material. Available via Monotype's distribution network through Fonts.com, FontShop and MyFonts.
foundry_blurb: The Foundry Types describe Architype Aubette as part of their Architype Konstrukt collection, a set of avant-garde typefaces derived from the work of artists and designers of the inter-war years whose ideals shaped European modernism. The face is based on Van Doesburg's lettering for the 1928 inauguration of the Café Aubette in Strasbourg, a project with Jean and Sophie Arp that exemplified De Stijl theory at architectural scale.

name: Architype Schwitters
classification_notes: Derived from Kurt Schwitters' 1927 phonetic universal alphabet, this typeface uses heavier, wider, rounded forms to link sound and visual shape, placing it firmly in the experimental avant-garde display category.
summary: A revival of Kurt Schwitters' 1927 phonetic alphabet, where rounded and weighted letterforms were designed to mirror the sounds of vowels and consonants.
description: Part of The Foundry Types' Architype Konstrukt collection, this face is drawn from Schwitters' Systemschrift of 1927, an experiment in tying visual form to phonetic sound. Heavier, wider, rounded characters represent vowels, giving the alphabet a distinctive rhythmic visual texture. Character sets are intentionally limited owing to the experimental nature of the source material.
foundry_blurb: The Foundry Types describe Architype Schwitters as part of the Architype Konstrukt collection, a series of avant-garde typefaces derived from inter-war artists and designers whose ideas shaped European modernism. This face is based on Kurt Schwitters' 1927 universal alphabet, in which he explored linking sound to shape through heavier, wider, rounded forms for vowels, producing what they call a unique visual speech texture.

name: Architype Van Doesburg
designer: Theo Van Doesburg (original), The Foundry Types (revival)
classification_notes: Derived from a 1919 geometric alphabet built on a strict 5x5 grid, with forms dictated by De Stijl rectangularity theory. The limited character set and avant-garde origins place it firmly in the display/experimental category.
summary: A digitisation of Theo Van Doesburg's 1919 geometric alphabet, reconstructed on its original 5x5 grid with the character set kept deliberately limited to honour his single-alphabet theory.
description: Part of The Foundry Types' Architype Konstrukt collection, this typeface recovers Van Doesburg's experimental inter-war lettering, itself a direct expression of De Stijl's commitment to strict rectangularity. The forms are built entirely on a 5x5 grid, and the restricted character set reflects Van Doesburg's original single-alphabet concept rather than any failure of the revival. Historical samples from 1919 and 1920 publications, including 'Klei' magazine and 'La Section d'Or', document the source material.
foundry_blurb: The Foundry Types describe Architype Van Doesburg as part of their Architype Konstrukt collection, a set of avant-garde typefaces drawn from the work of inter-war artists and designers whose ideals shaped European modernism. This face derives from Van Doesburg's 1919 geometric alphabet, constructed on a 5x5 grid and constrained by his single-alphabet theory, with De Stijl's principle of rectangularity governing every form.

name: Architype Van der Leck
classification_notes: Derived from De Stijl lettering with restricted, stencil-like geometric forms rooted in early 20th-century avant-garde art; classified as display due to its highly abstract and experimental character construction.
summary: A typeface revival drawn from Bart Van der Leck's 1941 lettering for 'Het Vlas' magazine, capturing the stencil-like geometric rigour of De Stijl.
description: Part of The Foundry Types' Architype Konstrukt collection, this face recovers the abstract, angular letterforms Van der Leck produced for a 1941 Dutch flax publication. The restricted shapes echo De Stijl's hard geometric language and connect directly to his contemporaneous abstract paintings. Character sets are limited by design, reflecting the experimental nature of the source material.
foundry_blurb: The Foundry Types describe Architype Van der Leck as part of their Architype Konstrukt collection, a set of avant-garde typefaces drawn from the work of inter-war artists and designers whose ideals shaped European modernism. This face originates from lettering Bart Van der Leck created for 'Het Vlas' magazine in 1941, with abstract, stencil-like forms reflecting the geometric language of De Stijl and his abstract paintings.

name: Architype Ballmer
classification_notes: A geometric display type derived from Theo Ballmer's Bauhaus-era universal letterforms, built on a strict grid with De Stijl square forms. Its single-alphabet, avant-garde structure places it firmly in early modernist display territory rather than any conventional text category.
summary: A digital revival of Theo Ballmer's 1928 grid-based universal letterforms, drawn for his Bauhaus-influenced Swiss exhibition posters.
description: Part of The Foundry Types' Architype Universal collection, this face reconstructs the strict square, grid-governed letterforms Ballmer devised for a series of 1928 Swiss posters, including his well-known 'Norm' exhibition piece for the Kunstgewerbemuseum in Zürich. The single-alphabet theory of the interwar avant-garde limits the character set, making this a specialist display type rather than a workhorse. Its references to De Stijl geometry and universal alphabet ideals give it a precise historical footing within early European modernism.
foundry_blurb: The Foundry Types describe Architype Ballmer as part of their Architype Universal collection, a set of avant-garde typefaces drawn from the work of interwar artists and designers whose ideas underpinned European modernism. This face is inspired by the experimental, universal letterforms Bauhaus-trained Swiss designer Theo Ballmer created for his 1928 exhibition posters, with grid-based square forms referencing De Stijl.

name: Architype Bayer
classification_notes: A revival of Herbert Bayer's 1925 'universal' single-alphabet Bauhaus design, built on strict geometric principles with minimal distinction between upper and lowercase forms.
summary: A revival of Herbert Bayer's 1925 Bauhaus 'universal' alphabet, originally conceived as a minimal single-case geometric sans that was never cut as a typeface in his lifetime.
description: Drawn from Bauhaus Archiv sketches, this typeface reconstructs Bayer's radical reduction of the Latin alphabet to a single case, stripping out all but the most essential geometric forms. It belongs to The Foundry Types' Architype Universal collection, a series of revivals rooted in the inter-war European modernist movement. The character set is deliberately limited, faithful to the theoretical constraints of the original 'universal alphabet' thesis.
foundry_blurb: The Foundry Types describe Architype Bayer as part of their Architype Universal collection, a set of avant-garde typefaces derived from the work of artists and designers of the inter-war years whose ideas underpin European modernism. The typeface was drawn from Bauhaus Archiv sketches of Bayer's 1925 minimal sans, itself based on his single-alphabet student thesis and intended exclusively for Bauhaus use, though never produced as a typeface at the time.

name: Architype Bayer-Type
classification_notes: Based on Herbert Bayer's 1931 grid-based, geometrically constructed serifed alphabet, it sits within the geometric serif category despite its avant-garde, Bauhaus origins. The structure is rigorously grid-based, distinguishing it from traditional serif classifications.
summary: A revival of Herbert Bayer's 1931 geometrically constructed serif alphabet, part of The Foundry Types' Architype Universal collection of interwar modernist typefaces.
description: Drawn from Herbert Bayer's 1931 serifed universal alphabet, this is a geometrically structured type built on a strict grid, extending Bayer's earlier 1925 sans-serif single alphabet into a serif form. Part of the Architype Universal collection, which revives the single-alphabet, universal-theory work of European Bauhaus and modernist designers from the interwar period. The limited character set reflects the ideological constraints of the original universal alphabet theory.
foundry_blurb: Architype Bayer-Type is part of The Foundry Types' Architype Universal collection, a set of avant-garde typefaces derived from the work of interwar artists and designers whose ideals underpin European modernism. Based on Herbert Bayer's 1931 universal serif alphabet, the design is grid-based and geometrically constructed, continuing the structural logic of his earlier 1925 sans single alphabet despite its apparent stylistic departure.

name: Architype Bill
classification_notes: Derived from Max Bill's Bauhaus-influenced letterforms constructed from straight lines and triangles on a mathematical basis, placing it firmly in geometric display territory with a single-weight, limited character set consistent with avant-garde universal alphabet principles.
summary: A geometric typeface reconstructed from Max Bill's 1949 exhibition poster lettering, built almost entirely from straight lines and triangles on a mathematical framework.
description: Part of The Foundry's Architype Universal collection, this face reconstructs letterforms Max Bill developed for a 1944 poster, extending them into a working typeface while preserving the strict constructivist logic of the originals. Nearly every character is derived from straight lines and triangles alone, with only the letter 'o' departing from that rule. The single-style family reflects the universal alphabet theory shared by Bauhaus-era designers, resulting in a deliberately limited but historically grounded character set.
foundry_blurb: The Foundry describe Architype Bill as part of the Architype Universal collection, a series of avant-garde typefaces drawn from the work of inter-war artists and designers whose ideals shaped European modernism. The face was developed from the few letterforms Max Bill created for a 1949 exhibition poster, with almost all forms constructed from straight lines and triangles on a purely mathematical basis reflecting his Bauhaus training and the universal alphabet principle.

name: Architype Tschichold
classification_notes: A single-alphabet geometric construction from the Bauhaus-era avant-garde tradition, with only one case and phonetic characters; best classified as display due to its highly experimental, art-movement-specific form.
summary: A digital rendering of Jan Tschichold's 1929 single-alphabet experimental typeface, part of The Foundry's Architype Universal collection of interwar avant-garde designs.
description: Based on Tschichold's geometrically constructed 1929 alphabet, itself influenced by Herbert Bayer's universal type, this face was never put into commercial production in its own time. The Foundry's revival faithfully reconstructs the original, including its phonetic characters and the stark logic of its single-case system. It sits within the Architype Universal collection, which gathers surviving experimental alphabets from the interwar European modernist movement.
foundry_blurb: The Foundry describe Architype Tschichold as a faithful rendering of Jan Tschichold's 1929 experimental single alphabet, itself influenced by Bayer's universal theory. Part of the Architype Universal collection, it reconstructs Tschichold's original geometrically constructed design, which was never put into production, including its phonetic characters. The collection as a whole draws from avant-garde designers of the interwar years whose ideals shaped European modernism.
